title: Unscented sensitive-skin cold-process soap
subtitle: A plain, fragrance-free bar with a high olive content and generous superfat
excerpt: A minimal cold-process bar made for people who react to fragrance or need a very gentle daily wash. High olive oil content for conditioning, a modest coconut oil for lather, and an 8% superfat for extra mildness. No colouring, no essential oils.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 8% superfat for extra conditioning; water 33% of oil weight. High-olive, fragrance-free formulation recommended for sensitive and reactive skin in Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), and in standard dermatology guidance on syndets and plain-soap alternatives.

recipe.batchNotes: Run all changes through a lye calculator. The 8% superfat is intentional; do not reduce it.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 48 hours in the mould. This bar benefits from a full 6-week cure; the high olive oil content means the bar is quite soft until fully cured.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store on an open rack in a cool, dark, dry place. Fully cured bars keep two years. High-olive bars have a stable fatty acid profile and are less prone to DOS than high-linoleic formulas. Discard on orange oily spots or a paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between fat and sodium hydroxide that produces soap and glycerine. Each oil has a SAP value: the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The percentage of fat deliberately left unsaponified in the bar. At 8% superfat, the bar is particularly mild and conditioning. It is especially suited to sensitive or reactive skin.
The point at which soap batter thickens to a loose custard, indicating the oils and lye have emulsified. High-olive batters trace more slowly than coconut-dominant batches.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: High-olive bars can take longer to firm. Wait 3 days; if the loaf still feels sticky, leave another day before cutting. Wear protective gloves when cutting.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: At 6 weeks the bar is firm, very white, and thoroughly mild. Test a sliver on the forearm; there should be no sting at all.

content.text: There is a lot of soap that is not for sensitive skin, and not much that is. This bar is designed specifically for people who react to fragrance, essential oils, or cosmetic additives: no colour, no scent, no botanicals. The formula uses 65% olive oil for a gentle, conditioning lather that does not strip the skin, 25% coconut oil for enough cleansing power and foam to work as a daily wash, and 10% castor oil to stabilise the lather. The
superfat

is set at 8%, higher than the standard 5%, which leaves a larger reserve of free conditioning oil in the bar. The trade-off is a slightly softer bar that takes longer to cure; the full 6-week cure is important here. The lather is fine-bubbled and creamy rather than voluminous; this is the character of high-olive soap and is not a fault. Yield is 7 bars of around 120 g each.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before opening the lye container.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 8% superfat on this oil blend

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a ventilated space with protective gloves and goggles on. Weigh the sodium hydroxide. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream while stirring. Never add water to lye. The solution heats sharply; step back. Leave to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the oils
Melt the coconut oil in a heatproof bowl over low heat. Add the olive oil and castor oil. Stir to combine. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Combine and blend
Pour the lye solution into the oil blend in a slow stream. Use the stick blender in short 5-second bursts, alternating with hand-stirring, until the batter reaches light
trace
. High-olive batters trace slowly; this may take 3 to 5 minutes of intermittent blending. Pour once light trace is reached. The
saponification
reaction in a high-olive bar is gentler and produces less heat than a coconut-dominant batch.
Pour, insulate, unmould, and cure

Pour into the silicone loaf mould, smooth the top, wrap in a towel, and leave undisturbed for 2 to 3 days. Turn out after 3 days (or leave longer if still soft). Cut into bars wearing protective gloves. Cure on an open rack in a cool dry room for 4 to 6 weeks. Turn the bars every two weeks.
Storage and shelf life

Store on an open rack in a cool, dark, dry place. In good conditions, fully cured bars keep two years. High-olive soaps are among the more stable formulations and are less prone to DOS than high-linoleic or nut-oil bars. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots or a sharp smell that does not belong.
